groups.text: It is currently 24 degrees and partly cloudy in Berlin-Tempelhof.
With the morning weather set, here is the news.

Palantir has introduced a new intelligent engine designed to serve U.S. government agencies by integrating NVIDIA Nemotron open models. This system allows the government to build custom, frontier-quality models within air-gapped environments that remain completely isolated from unsecured networks. By using these open models on NVIDIA accelerated computing, agencies can maintain strict control over proprietary data and model weights in sensitive settings. Separately, new research from Google and Public First highlights a stark divide in how artificial intelligence is impacting the British workforce. While workplace AI adoption in the UK has doubled over the last year to 73 percent, the benefits remain uneven. Only the top 15 percent of users, labeled as AI Trailblazers, report substantial time savings, pay increases, and stronger performance reviews. This leaves a significant challenge for the remaining 85 percent of workers who are still in the early stages of adoption.

The rise of autonomous AI agents creates a tension between productivity and security. These agents now move beyond simple chat to inspect code, run tests, and query internal systems for hours on behalf of a user. While this increases efficiency, NVIDIA warns that it grants agents access to sensitive enterprise data and the ability to take actions across business systems, making governed environments essential. Separately, Google is emphasizing a full-stack approach to AI infrastructure. Expert Richard Seroter explains that integrating every layer—from hardware and models to user interfaces—into one cohesive system improves reliability and lowers costs. This strategy removes the need to stitch together parts from multiple vendors. Developers can currently implement this through Google AI Studio for prototypes, the Gemini Enterprise Platform for automation, or the Antigravity platform for building complex agents.

Meta is restricting its engineers' use of Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex to prevent rival AI outputs from leaking into Meta's own training data. Internal documents show the company has temporarily halted some work with these models to avoid distillation, which is the unauthorized transfer of capabilities between systems. This move comes as Meta builds its own coding assistant, MetaCode, while facing billions of dollars in internal AI costs this year. Meanwhile, Microsoft Research has introduced Memora, a scalable memory system designed to solve the problem of AI agents being effectively stateless. Unlike current models that must repeatedly reload context or retrieve information from external sources, Memora decouples stored content from how it is retrieved using lightweight abstractions. This allows agents to handle long-horizon tasks more efficiently, outperforming RAG and full-context inference while using up to 98 percent fewer context tokens.

AI startups are increasingly moving away from external models to build their own proprietary technology for better long-term defensibility. Base44, the vibe coding platform acquired by Wix for 80 million dollars, has begun rolling out its own AI model to help users create apps via natural language. Founder Maor Shlomo says owning the entire stack allows for greater optimizations in cost, latency, and efficiency compared to relying on frontier models. This shift highlights a broader tension over whether businesses built on third-party models can remain competitive against rivals like Lovable. Separately, researchers at Ai2 have introduced DiScoFormer, a transformer-based estimator designed to recover data distributions from finite samples. Unlike classical kernel density estimation, which loses accuracy as dimensionality grows, DiScoFormer can infer both the distribution's density and its score in a single forward pass. This allows it to remain accurate in high-dimensional settings without requiring retraining for every new distribution.

Machine learning researchers face a persistent trade-off between generalizability and accuracy when trying to recover data distributions. To solve this, a new transformer called DiScoFormer estimates two critical quantities: the distribution's density and its score. Density acts as a smooth version of a histogram to show where data points cluster. The score is the gradient of the log-density, which points toward more probable regions. This specific mechanism drives Bayesian sampling and particle simulations used to model systems like plasma. It also powers diffusion-based generative models, such as DALL-E and Stable Diffusion, by turning random noise into realistic images. While classical kernel density estimation requires no training and applies to any distribution, its accuracy drops sharply as dimensionality increases. DiScoFormer aims to bridge this gap by providing a single transformer capable of handling both density and score across various distributions.

Senator Elizabeth Warren and Representative Mary Gay Scanlon are planning to debut a new version of the Health and Location Data Protection Act. This proposal would ban the sale of Americans' health and location information to data brokers, including sensitive details users reveal to AI chatbots like ChatGPT or Claude. The updated bill expands on a 2022 version by prohibiting other companies from selling this specific data to brokers. Separately, Anthropic’s Claude models are now generally available in Microsoft Foundry. These models run on NVIDIA GB300 Blackwell Ultra GPUs and are hosted on Microsoft Azure. This infrastructure allows Azure-native enterprises to build autonomous, domain-specific AI agents using NVIDIA Quantum-X800 InfiniBand networking. Companies can further govern these autonomous systems by using the NVIDIA Secure Agent Workspace Reference Design to control identity and network access at the infrastructure level.

Palantir is introducing a new intelligent engine designed specifically for the needs of United States government agencies. The system focuses on bringing secure AI into closed environments by utilizing NVIDIA Nemotron open models. This move highlights a strategic reliance on open source innovation to build tools for American federal operations. By integrating these specific open models, Palantir aims to provide a level of security and flexibility required for high-stakes agency work. It is a concrete example of how open architecture is being adapted for the most restrictive government settings.
After the headlines, back to the weather.
This afternoon will be cloudy with rain chances up to 53 percent and temperatures between 19 and 28 degrees.
And from the weather, a quick look at the calendar.

On your calendar today: Your work calendar has weekly lunch at noon, Felix and Patrick at one in the afternoon, NAS sync at two, and a RAYX meeting at three. Your personal calendar has Krebs at six in the evening.
Mail worth checking: OpenAI sent a security alert about your MFA settings. You also have an urgent message from HEIBRiDS recruitment and a meeting agenda from Synje Jungbehr.
And that's it for your personal morning broadcast.
[mature adult voice, intensely emotional, tender, radiant; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
[very very long pause before speaking; then mature adult voice, most emotional, strong, warm, radiant, deeply encouraging; hold a long breath after Rise] Rise... and shine.
